He published his findings in 1929. Howard Florey and Ernst Chain later developed penicillin into a clinical antibiotic in the 1940s.

claim: The Treaty of Versailles was signed in 1919, formally ending World War I.
context: The Treaty of Versailles was signed on June 28, 1919, exactly five years after the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand, in the Hall of Mirrors at the Palace of Versailles. It formally ended the state of war between the Allied Powers and Germany.

claim: Charles Darwin and Alfred Russell Wallace independently developed the theory of natural selection.
context: Darwin and Wallace both independently arrived at the theory of natural selection. Their papers were jointly presented to the Linnean Society of London on July 1, 1858. Darwin's On the Origin of Species, published in 1859, provided the comprehensive evidence base for the theory.

claim: Neil Armstrong was the first human to walk on the Moon on July 20, 1969.
context: Neil Armstrong became the first human to step onto the lunar surface at 02:56 UTC on July 21, 1969 (July 20 in US time zones), during the Apollo 11 mission. His first words were 'That's one small step for [a] man, one giant leap for mankind.'

claim: Albert Einstein received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1921 for his discovery of the photoelectric effect.
context: Albert Einstein was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics for the year 1921 (announced in 1922) for his discovery of the law of the photoelectric effect. He was not awarded it for the theory of relativity, as the Nobel Committee required more empirical confirmation at that time.

claim: Marie Curie was the first person to win Nobel Prizes in two different scientific fields.
context: Marie Curie won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1903 (shared with Pierre Curie and Henri Becquerel) for research on radioactivity, and the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1911 for the discovery of radium and polonium. She remains the only person to have won Nobel Prizes in two different sciences.

claim: The human genome contains approximately 3 billion base pairs.
context: The human haploid genome contains approximately 3.2 billion base pairs of DNA distributed across 23 chromosomes. The Human Genome Project, completed in 2003, produced the first reference sequence of the human genome.

claim: The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989.
context: The Berlin Wall fell on the night of November 9-10, 1989, after East Germany's Communist government announced that citizens could cross the border freely. Crowds dismantled sections of the wall, and it became one of the defining moments of the end of the Cold War.

claim: Facebook was founded by Mark Zuckerberg in 2004 while he was a student at Harvard.
context: Facebook was launched on February 4, 2004, by Mark Zuckerberg along with co-founders Eduardo Saverin, Andrew McCollum, Dustin Moskovitz, and Chris Hughes. Zuckerberg was a sophomore at Harvard at the time.

claim: Google was founded in 1998 by Larry Page and Sergey Brin.
context: Google was incorporated on September 4, 1998, by Larry Page and Sergey Brin while they were PhD students at Stanford University. The name is a play on the mathematical term 'googol' (10¹⁰⁰).

claim: The Higgs boson was confirmed at CERN's Large Hadron Collider in July 2012.
context: On July 4, 2012, CERN announced that both the ATLAS and CMS experiments had independently observed a new particle consistent with the Higgs boson at a mass of approximately 125-126 GeV. Peter Higgs and François Englert were awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2013 for theorizing it.

claim: Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web in 1989.
context: Tim Berners-Lee proposed the World Wide Web in a March 1989 proposal titled 'Information Management: A Proposal.' He developed the first web server, browser, and website at CERN. The first website went live on August 6, 1991.

claim: The Paris Agreement on climate change was adopted on December 12, 2015.
context: The Paris Agreement was adopted at the UN Climate Conference (COP21) in Paris on December 12, 2015. It was signed on April 22, 2016, and entered into force on November 4, 2016, after ratification by a sufficient number of countries.

claim: The WHO declared COVID-19 a pandemic on March 11, 2020.
context: WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus declared COVID-19 a pandemic on March 11, 2020, noting that in the preceding two weeks the number of cases outside China had increased 13-fold. The virus was first identified in Wuhan, China in December 2019.

claim: The human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons.
context: A 2009 study by Azevedo et al. using the isotropic fractionator method found that the human brain contains approximately 86 billion neurons and approximately 85 billion non-neuronal cells. Earlier estimates of 100 billion neurons were based on extrapolation.

claim: Mount Everest is Earth's highest mountain above sea level, measuring approximately 8,849 meters.
context: Mount Everest in the Himalayas is Earth's highest peak above sea level. A 2020 Chinese-Nepali joint survey remeasured it at 8,848.86 meters (commonly rounded to 8,849 m), slightly higher than the previous official measurement of 8,848 m.

claim: Watson and Crick published the double helix structure of DNA in Nature in April 1953.
context: Watson and Crick's paper 'Molecular Structure of Nucleic Acids: A Structure for Deoxyribose Nucleic Acid' was published in Nature on April 25, 1953. The same issue included supporting papers by Rosalind Franklin and Raymond Gosling, and by Maurice Wilkins, Alec Stokes, and Herbert Wilson.

claim: Yuri Gagarin became the first human to travel to space on April 12, 1961.
context: Yuri Gagarin of the Soviet Union completed the first human spaceflight on April 12, 1961, aboard Vostok 1. He completed one orbit of the Earth in 108 minutes. April 12 is now celebrated internationally as Yuri's Night.

claim: The first successful human heart transplant was performed by Dr. Christiaan Barnard in 1967 in South Africa.
context: Dr. Christiaan Barnard performed the first human-to-human heart transplant on December 3, 1967, at Groote Schuur Hospital in Cape Town. The recipient, Louis Washkansky, survived 18 days before dying of pneumonia.

claim: The Amazon River has the largest discharge of any river in the world.
context: The Amazon River discharges approximately 209,000 cubic meters of water per second into the Atlantic Ocean — more than the next seven largest rivers combined, representing about 20% of all freshwater discharged into the world's oceans. The Nile is longer, but the Amazon has far greater discharge.

claim: The liver produces bile, which is stored in the gallbladder and released to aid fat digestion.
context: The liver continuously produces bile, which is transported to the gallbladder for storage and concentration. When fatty food enters the small intestine, the gallbladder releases bile into the duodenum to emulsify fats and facilitate their digestion.

claim: Mitochondria produce ATP through oxidative phosphorylation.
context: Mitochondria generate most of the cell's ATP via oxidative phosphorylation, which uses the electron transport chain in the inner mitochondrial membrane to create a proton gradient that drives ATP synthase. This produces approximately 30-32 ATP molecules per glucose molecule.

claim: The Coriolis effect causes large-scale atmospheric circulation patterns including trade winds and cyclones.
context: The Coriolis effect, arising from Earth's rotation, deflects moving air masses to the right in the Northern Hemisphere and to the left in the Southern Hemisphere. This deflection drives the formation of trade winds, westerlies, polar easterlies, and cyclonic/anticyclonic circulation patterns.

claim: The Treaty of Westphalia (1648) established the modern concept of state sovereignty.
context: The Peace of Westphalia (1648) ended the Thirty Years' War and is widely cited as establishing the principle of state sovereignty — that each state has supreme authority within its own territory and no external power has the right to interfere in its domestic affairs. This framework underpins the modern international system.

claim: Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid) works by inhibiting cyclooxygenase (COX) enzymes, which reduces prostaglandin synthesis.
context: Aspirin irreversibly inhibits both COX-1 and COX-2 enzymes by acetylating a serine residue. This reduces prostaglandin and thromboxane synthesis, producing analgesic, antipyretic, anti-inflammatory, and antiplatelet effects. This mechanism was elucidated by John Vane, who won the Nobel Prize in 1982.

claim: Penicillin works by inhibiting bacterial cell wall synthesis.
context: Penicillin and related beta-lactam antibiotics inhibit the transpeptidase enzymes (penicillin-binding proteins) that cross-link peptidoglycan in the bacterial cell wall. This prevents cell wall formation, causing the bacterium to lyse under osmotic pressure.

claim: The ozone layer absorbs approximately 97-99% of the Sun's ultraviolet radiation.
context: The stratospheric ozone layer, located 15-35 km above Earth's surface, absorbs approximately 97-99% of the Sun's medium-frequency UV light (UV-B: 280-315 nm and UV-C: 100-280 nm). The depletion of this layer by chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) was a major environmental concern addressed by the Montreal Protocol (1987).

claim: DNA replication requires the enzyme DNA polymerase, which synthesizes new DNA strands in the 5' to 3' direction.
context: DNA polymerase is the primary enzyme for DNA synthesis. It can only add nucleotides in the 5' to 3' direction, requiring a primer (provided by primase) and reading the template strand 3' to 5'. This directionality creates the leading and lagging strand distinction during replication.

claim: The pancreas produces both insulin and glucagon to regulate blood glucose levels.
context: The endocrine cells of the pancreas (islets of Langerhans) produce insulin (beta cells) and glucagon (alpha cells). Insulin lowers blood glucose by promoting cellular uptake; glucagon raises it by stimulating glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis in the liver.

claim: Nelson Mandela was imprisoned on Robben Island for 18 of his 27 years in prison.
context: Nelson Mandela was sentenced to life imprisonment in 1964 following the Rivonia Trial. He spent 18 of his 27 years in prison on Robben Island, then was transferred to Pollsmoor Prison in 1982 and to Victor Verster Prison in 1988, where he was released on February 11, 1990.

claim: Gravity accelerates all objects toward Earth at approximately 9.8 m/s² regardless of their mass, in the absence of air resistance.
context: In the absence of air resistance, all objects near Earth's surface accelerate due to gravity at approximately 9.80665 m/s² (standard g), regardless of their mass. This was demonstrated by Galileo's experiments (debunking Aristotle) and is a consequence of the equivalence of gravitational and inertial mass.

claim: The human heart generates its own electrical impulses through the sinoatrial (SA) node, which acts as the natural pacemaker.
context: The sinoatrial node in the right atrium is the heart's natural pacemaker, spontaneously generating electrical impulses at 60-100 times per minute in healthy adults. These impulses propagate through the conduction system (AV node, Bundle of His, Purkinje fibers) to coordinate the heartbeat.
